![Monitoring dashboard for Class Loading](_images/monitoring-dashboards-classloading.png)

Monitor class loading to:

- Identify classloader memory leaks
- Optimize application startup performance
- Analyze dynamic code loading patterns

## Server Group and Cluster Monitoring Dashboard

Monitor distributed infrastructure performance through aggregated dashboards that provide unified
visibility across multiple servers organized in groups or clusters.

### Overview

Server group and cluster monitoring enables centralized performance analysis across your distributed
hybrid standalone infrastructure, providing:

- Combined view of CPU, memory, and heap utilization across all servers
- Side-by-side performance comparison between individual servers
- Load distribution patterns across server infrastructure
- Collective resource utilization for infrastructure planning

The group and cluster dashboard displays essential aggregated infrastructure metrics:

- CPU Usage: Aggregate processing capacity utilization across all servers (percentage)
- Memory Usage: Combined memory consumption across the infrastructure (MB)
- Heap Total: Aggregate JVM heap allocation for all servers (MB)
- Individual Server Breakdown: Per-server performance within the group context

Aggregated metrics appear as unified plot lines representing the collective performance of selected
servers.

### Access Group and Cluster Dashboards

To monitor aggregated performance metrics for your server infrastructure:

1.  In Runtime Manager, click **Servers**.
2.  Click the server group or cluster name to display the aggregated dashboard.
3.  Select specific servers from the list to display comparative metrics in the details pane.

### Dashboard Navigation

- Time Range Selection

Monitor performance trends across different time periods:

![Monitoring dashboard for a server group or cluster](_images/monitoring-dashboards-cbd92.png)

Choose from available time ranges: \* **3 Hours**: Real-time infrastructure monitoring \* **24
Hours**: Recent performance analysis \* **7 Days**: Weekly infrastructure trends \* **2 Weeks**:
Historical capacity analysis

- Server-Focused View

Access server-specific performance data within the group context:

![Monitoring dashboard for a server group or cluster](_images/monitoring-dashboards-6fe9f.png)

From the group dashboard, navigate to individual server dashboards by clicking server names to
access detailed performance metrics and troubleshooting information.

### Troubleshooting Server Group Monitoring

If monitoring metrics donât appear immediately after configuring Anypoint Monitoring for servers in
a server group, follow this systematic installation process:

1.  In Anypoint Platform go to **Runtime Manager** > **Servers** > **Add Server** to create an
    Anypoint Runtime Manager token.
2.  Execute the command provided in the **Add Server** screen.
3.  In Anypoint Platform go to **Runtime Manager** > **Servers** > **Create Group** to create the
    server group.
4.  Start Mule for all the servers to be added to the server group by using the command
    `./mule start` from the `$MULE_HOME/bin` directory.
5.  In Anypoint Platform go to **Runtime Manager** > **Servers**, click the server group name, and
    **Add Server** to add all the servers to the group.
6.  Deploy an API to the server group.
7.  Stop any running filebeat process in all the servers of the server group and run the `./install`
    command from the `$MULE_HOME/am/bin` directory in all the servers of the server group.
8.  In Anypoint Platform go to **Runtime Manager** > **Servers**, click the server group name, and
    restart all the servers in the server group via **Actions** > **Restart**.
9.  Run `./setup` from the `$MULE_HOME/am/bin` for all the servers in the server group.

    If after this step the metrics donât show up, repeat the last two steps.

## See Also

- [Monitoring Hybrid Standalone Instances](monitoring-hybrid-standalone)
- [Creating Alerts for Servers, Server Groups, and Clusters](creating-alerts-hybrid)
- [Managing Hybrid Standalone Instances](managing-hybrid-standalone)
- [Managing Servers](managing-servers)
- [Managing Server Groups](managing-servers-groups)
- [Managing Clusters](managing-clusters)
---

# Monitoring Hybrid Standalone Instances

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Monitor your hybrid standalone instances to ensure optimal performance, maintain system health, and
enable proactive problem resolution across your distributed Mule runtime environment.

Runtime Manager provides comprehensive monitoring capabilities for your hybrid standalone
infrastructure through integrated dashboards and alerting systems.

## Creating Alerts

Set up alerts to get email notifications when servers go down, use too much memory or CPU, or when
applications fail. This helps you fix problems quickly.

[Creating Alerts for Server, Server Groups or Clusters](creating-alerts-hybrid)

## Monitoring Dashboards

View charts and graphs to see how much CPU and memory your servers are using, check application
performance, and spot problems before they become serious.

[Monitoring Dashboards for Server, Server Groups and Clusters](monitoring-dashboards-hybrid)

> [!IMPORTANT] When running Mule on Windows systems, the Mule service must be started by a user
> account that belongs to the **Performance Log Users** group to enable proper dashboard
> functionality.

## See Also

- [Managing Hybrid Standalone Instances](managing-hybrid-standalone)
- [Setting up Hybrid Standalone Instances](setting-hybrid-standalone)
- [Deploying Mule Apps to Hybrid Standalone Instances](deploying-apps-hybrid-standalone)
---

# Setting up Hybrid Standalone Instances

> For the full documentation index, see: https://docs.mulesoft.com/llms.txt

Set up hybrid standalone instances to enable centralized management and deployment of Mule
applications through Anypoint Platform while maintaining control over your infrastructure.

## Overview

The setup process includes:

- Prepare infrastructure and plan deployment
- Install and configure Mule runtime engine (Mule)
- Install and set up Runtime Manager agent
- Create servers, server groups, and clusters with Anypoint Platform
- Configure and validate initial setup

## Prepare Infrastructure

Before setting up hybrid standalone instances, make sure your infrastructure meets these
requirements:

- Allocate adequate CPU, memory, and storage for your Mule runtime instances
- Configure outbound HTTPS connectivity (port 443) and firewall rules for Runtime Manager agent
- Use supported OS versions (Linux, Windows, macOS) with compatible Java versions
- Obtain Anypoint Platform subscription

## Install and Configure Mule Runtime

Set up your Mule runtime environment:

- Download and install Mule runtime engine  
  Download the appropriate Mule version and set up your environment with proper configuration. For
  detailed installation instructions, see
  [Download, Install, Configure, and Upgrade Mule](../mule-runtime/latest/runtime-installation-task).
- Configure runtime properties and settings  
  Configure memory settings, environment variables, and connection properties through the
  `wrapper.conf` file. For configuration options, see
  [Configuring Properties](../mule-runtime/latest/configuring-properties) and
  [Mule Configuration File](../mule-runtime/latest/about-mule-configuration).
- Install enterprise license  
  Install your MuleSoft Enterprise license for production deployments. See
  [Install an Enterprise License](../mule-runtime/latest/installing-an-enterprise-license) for
  complete instructions.
- Run Mule as a service  
  Configure Mule as a system service for automatic startup and management. See
  [Download, Install, Configure, and Upgrade Mule](../mule-runtime/latest/runtime-installation-task)
  for service installation instructions.
- Harden your installation  
  Apply security best practices for production environments. See
  [Hardening your Mule Installation](../mule-runtime/latest/hardening-your-mule-installation) for
  security configuration guidance.

## Install and Set Up Runtime Manager Agent

The Runtime Manager agent is a lightweight component that enables communication between your
on-premises Mule runtime instances and Anypoint Platform in the cloud.

With the Runtime Manager agent, you can:

- Register servers with Anypoint Platform for centralized management
- Deploy and manage applications remotely through Runtime Manager
- Monitor server health, performance metrics, and application status
- Configure server properties and environment variables
- Receive real-time alerts and notifications
- Access application logs and diagnostic information

For detailed configuration and setup instructions, refer to
[Runtime Manager Agent](../runtime-manager/runtime-manager-agent).

## Create Servers, Server Groups, and Clusters

Use Runtime Manager to create your servers with Anypoint Platform, enabling centralized management
and deployment. Once registered, you can organize servers into groups or clusters and deploy
applications to them.

### Servers

[Creating Servers](creating-servers)  
Individual server instances that provide a single deployment target for Mule applications. Servers
are ideal for:

- Development and testing environments
- Simple deployments with minimal complexity
- Scenarios where you need direct control over individual server instances
- Applications that donât require high availability or load distribution

Each server runs independently and can be managed separately through Runtime Manager.

### Server Groups

[Creating Server Groups](creating-servers-groups)  
Multiple servers organized together to act as a single deployment target. Server groups are ideal
for:

- Production environments requiring redundancy without cluster complexity
- Scenarios where you need to deploy applications to multiple servers simultaneously
- Load distribution across multiple servers for increased traffic handling
- Centralized management of properties, monitoring, and alerts
- Applications that can run independently on each server without shared state

Each server in the group runs its own copy of the application, providing redundancy if one server
fails.

### Clusters

[Creating Clusters](creating-clusters)  
High-availability configuration where multiple servers work together as a unified system. Clusters
are ideal for:

- Mission-critical applications requiring maximum availability and performance
- Scenarios where automatic failover and load balancing are essential
- Applications that need synchronized communication and shared state
- Zero-downtime deployments and rolling updates
- High-traffic environments requiring automatic traffic distribution

Cluster members work together as a unified system with automatic failover and load balancing.

All servers within a server group or cluster must have identical versions of:

- Mule runtime engine
- Runtime Manager agent

### Registration Workflow

1.  Use Runtime Manager to register each server and configure its properties
2.  Add registered servers to a server group or cluster based on your requirements
3.  Select the individual server, server group, or cluster for your application deployments

## Configure and Validate Initial Setup

Verify your hybrid standalone configuration:

- Test network connectivity to Anypoint Platform and verify DNS resolution
- Confirm Runtime Manager agent is running and communicating with Anypoint Platform
- Verify servers appear in Runtime Manager with "Running" status
- Test server management operations and application deployment capabilities

## See Also

- [Creating Servers](creating-servers)
- [Creating Server Groups](creating-servers-groups)
- [Creating Clusters](creating-clusters)
- [Managing Hybrid Standalone Instances](managing-hybrid-standalone)
- [Monitoring Hybrid Standalone Instances](monitoring-hybrid-standalone)
- [Deploying Mule Apps to Hybrid Standalone Instances](deploying-apps-hybrid-standalone)
